# UC-14.4.14 · Sensor Data Gap Detection

## Description

Missing telemetry breaks automation and safety analytics; gaps often precede device failure.

## Value

Missing telemetry breaks automation and safety analytics; gaps often precede device failure.

## Implementation

Tune gap threshold per sensor class (critical vs ambient). Alert on gap or stepped decrease in message rate.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: MQTT/CoAP gateway logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="iot:telemetry"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Tune gap threshold per sensor class (critical vs ambient). Alert on gap or stepped decrease in message rate.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=iot sourcetype="iot:telemetry"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen by device_id, sensor_id
| eval gap_min=round((now()-last_seen)/60,1)
| where gap_min > 30
| table device_id, sensor_id, last_seen, gap_min
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sensor Data Gap Detection** — Missing telemetry breaks automation and safety analytics; gaps often precede device failure.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="iot:telemetry"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): MQTT/CoAP gateway logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: iot; **sourcetype**: iot:telemetry. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=iot, sourcetype="iot:telemetry". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by device_id, sensor_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **gap_min** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where gap_min > 30` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Sensor Data Gap Detection**): table device_id, sensor_id, last_seen, gap_min


Step 3 — Validate
Compare device counts, last-seen times, and health fields to the IoT platform console (for example AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, or Google Cloud IoT) for the same period. Verify API polling intervals and that offline rules match your operational definition.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (gaps), Heatmap (device × hour), Line chart (messages/min).

## SPL

```spl
index=iot sourcetype="iot:telemetry"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen by device_id, sensor_id
| eval gap_min=round((now()-last_seen)/60,1)
| where gap_min > 30
| table device_id, sensor_id, last_seen, gap_min
```

## Visualization

Table (gaps), Heatmap (device × hour), Line chart (messages/min).
